{{Infobox ancient site |name=Nishihakayama Kofun |image= |location={{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} |位置= |type=Square-shaped kofun |area=Approximately20m per side |built=5th century |Burial Facility=Iron artifact burial facility |Interred Persons= |burial goods=Numerous iron burial goods |Historic Site Designation= |Special Notes=Buried kofun |architectural_styles={{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} |part_of={{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076}} }} '''Nishihakayama Kofun''' (西墓山古墳) was a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in Aoyama, [[Fujidera City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The mound is presumed to have been square-shaped and is one of the kofun that typeed part of the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], a [[:en:World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Historic Site site]].
The existence of the kofun was confirmed during an excavation conducted due to redevelopment for residential housing. A row of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] and an iron artifact burial structure were discovered, revealing a wealth of iron burial goods.
== Overview == The kofun was located west of the {{ill|Makuyama Kofun|ja|墓山古墳|1=Hakayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makuyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakayama Kofun|qq=}}'s front section and just south of its associated {{ill|Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja|浄元寺山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a satellite mound. In 1988, plans for replacing a wooden apartment complex with a reinforced concrete building prompted an excavation. Although no remains of the mound or related structures had been identified in this area, its proximity to Jōgenjiyama Kofun suggested the possibility of associated features such as moats or embankments, warranting a preliminary investigation. Shortly after the excavation began, a row of haniwa and an iron artifact burial facility were discovered.
=== Internal burial === [[File:Nishihakayama Kofun Shutaibu.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main burial area}}{{small|Exhibited at the [[AICEL Shura Hall|Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center]].}}]] The iron artifact burial facility was arranged in two rows: east and west. The eastern row measured 5.2 meters in length and 70–80 cm in width, containing over 232 iron burial goods, including [[tachi]] swords, {{ill|tsurugi|ja|剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsurugi|1=Double-edged sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396047}} swords, short swords, spears, and halberds. The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.
=== Haniwa and Dating === A row of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} was unearthed, with 16 pieces discovered on the north side and 31 on the south. Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.
=== Function of the Kofun === The absence of a burial chamber suggests that Nishihakayama Kofun may have served as a ceremonial satellite mound (fukusōyō baizuka) rather than a primary burial site. Similar satellite mounds are known in the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], such as {{ill|Katomboyama Kofun|ja|カトンボ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katomboyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsumakairi Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|1=Tsukamawari Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumakairi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakamiyama Kofun|ja|七観山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, as well as in the Furuichi Kofun Group, such as {{ill|Ariyama Kofun|ja|アリ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ariyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nochū Kofun|ja|野中古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nochū Kofun|qq=}}. Although Nishihakayama Kofun no longer exists, a section of the iron artifact burial facility was preserved and is now exhibited at the {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|1=AICEL Shura Hall|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aisel Shura Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}} (officially, Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center).
== References == * Nishihakayama Kofun: Furuichi Kofun Group Research Report III, Fujidera City Board of Education, 1997.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/9,0,98,146.html About Nishihakayama Kofun] (Fujidera City)
== See Also == * [[AICEL Shura Hall]] * [[Igenoyama Kofun]]

But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Nishihakayama Kofun |image= |location={{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} |位置= |type=Square-shaped kofun |area=Approximately20m per side |built=5th century |Burial Facility=Iron artifact burial facility |Interred Persons= |burial goods=Numerous iron burial goods |Historic Site Designation= |Special Notes=Buried kofun |architectural_styles={{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} |part_of={{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076}} }} '''Nishihakayama Kofun''' (西墓山古墳) was a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in Aoyama, [[Fujidera City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. The mound is presumed to have been square-shaped and is one of the kofun that typeed part of the [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]], a [[:en:World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Historic Site site]].
The existence of the kofun was confirmed during an excavation conducted due to redevelopment for residential housing. A row of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] and an iron artifact burial structure were discovered, revealing a wealth of iron burial goods.
== Overview == The kofun was located west of the {{ill|Makuyama Kofun|ja|墓山古墳|1=Hakayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makuyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakayama Kofun|qq=}}'s front section and just south of its associated {{ill|Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja|浄元寺山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a satellite mound. In 1988, plans for replacing a wooden apartment complex with a reinforced concrete building prompted an excavation. Although no remains of the mound or related structures had been identified in this area, its proximity to Jōgenjiyama Kofun suggested the possibility of associated features such as moats or embankments, warranting a preliminary investigation. Shortly after the excavation began, a row of haniwa and an iron artifact burial facility were discovered.
=== Internal burial === [[File:Nishihakayama Kofun Shutaibu.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main burial area}}{{small|Exhibited at the [[AICEL Shura Hall|Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center]].}}]] The iron artifact burial facility was arranged in two rows: east and west. The eastern row measured 5.2 meters in length and 70–80 cm in width, containing over 232 iron burial goods, including [[tachi]] swords, {{ill|tsurugi|ja|剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsurugi|1=Double-edged sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396047}} swords, short swords, spears, and halberds. The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.
=== Haniwa and Dating === A row of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} was unearthed, with 16 pieces discovered on the north side and 31 on the south. Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.
=== Function of the Kofun === The absence of a burial chamber suggests that Nishihakayama Kofun may have served as a ceremonial satellite mound (fukusōyō baizuka) rather than a primary burial site. Similar satellite mounds are known in the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]], such as {{ill|Katomboyama Kofun|ja|カトンボ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katomboyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsumakairi Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|1=Tsukamawari Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumakairi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakamiyama Kofun|ja|七観山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, as well as in the Furuichi Kofun Group, such as {{ill|Ariyama Kofun|ja|アリ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ariyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nochū Kofun|ja|野中古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nochū Kofun|qq=}}. Although Nishihakayama Kofun no longer exists, a section of the iron artifact burial facility was preserved and is now exhibited at the {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|1=AICEL Shura Hall|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aisel Shura Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}} (officially, Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center).
== References == * Nishihakayama Kofun: Furuichi Kofun Group Research Report III, Fujidera City Board of Education, 1997.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/9,0,98,146.html About Nishihakayama Kofun] (Fujidera City)
== See Also == * [[AICEL Shura Hall]] * [[Igenoyama Kofun]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西墓山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西墓山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西墓山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11627531]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Nishihakayama Kofun |image= |location={{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} |位置= |type=Square-shaped kofun |area=Approximately20m per side |built=5th century |Burial Facility=Iron artifact burial facility |Interred Persons= |burial goods=Numerous iron burial goods |Historic Site Designation= |Special Notes=Buried kofun |architectural_styles={{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} |part_of={{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076}} }} '''Nishihakayama Kofun''' (西墓山古墳) was a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in Aoyama, [[Fujidera City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. The mound is presumed to have been square-shaped and is one of the kofun that typeed part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group}}, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Historic Site site}}.
The existence of the kofun was confirmed during an excavation conducted due to redevelopment for residential housing. A row of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} and an iron artifact burial structure were discovered, revealing a wealth of iron burial goods.
== Overview == The kofun was located west of the {{ill|Makuyama Kofun|ja|墓山古墳|1=Hakayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makuyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakayama Kofun|qq=}}'s front section and just south of its associated {{ill|Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja|浄元寺山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a satellite mound. In 1988, plans for replacing a wooden apartment complex with a reinforced concrete building prompted an excavation. Although no remains of the mound or related structures had been identified in this area, its proximity to Jōgenjiyama Kofun suggested the possibility of associated features such as moats or embankments, warranting a preliminary investigation. Shortly after the excavation began, a row of haniwa and an iron artifact burial facility were discovered.
=== Internal burial === [[File:Nishihakayama Kofun Shutaibu.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main burial area}}{{small|Exhibited at the [[AICEL Shura Hall|Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center]].}}]] The iron artifact burial facility was arranged in two rows: east and west. The eastern row measured 5.2 meters in length and 70–80 cm in width, containing over 232 iron burial goods, including [[tachi]] swords, {{ill|tsurugi|ja|剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsurugi|1=Double-edged sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396047}} swords, short swords, spears, and halberds. The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.
=== Haniwa and Dating === A row of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} was unearthed, with 16 pieces discovered on the north side and 31 on the south. Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.
=== Function of the Kofun === The absence of a burial chamber suggests that Nishihakayama Kofun may have served as a ceremonial satellite mound (fukusōyō baizuka) rather than a primary burial site. Similar satellite mounds are known in the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}, such as {{ill|Katomboyama Kofun|ja|カトンボ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katomboyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsumakairi Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|1=Tsukamawari Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumakairi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakamiyama Kofun|ja|七観山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, as well as in the Furuichi Kofun Group, such as {{ill|Ariyama Kofun|ja|アリ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ariyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nochū Kofun|ja|野中古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nochū Kofun|qq=}}. Although Nishihakayama Kofun no longer exists, a section of the iron artifact burial facility was preserved and is now exhibited at the {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|1=AICEL Shura Hall|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aisel Shura Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}} (officially, Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center).
== References == * Nishihakayama Kofun: Furuichi Kofun Group Research Report III, Fujidera City Board of Education, 1997.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/9,0,98,146.html About Nishihakayama Kofun] (Fujidera City)
== See Also == * [[AICEL Shura Hall]] * [[Igenoyama Kofun]]

Shortly after the excavation began, a row of haniwa and an iron artifact burial facility were discovered.
=== Internal burial === [[File:Nishihakayama Kofun Shutaibu.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main burial area}}{{small|Exhibited at the [[AICEL Shura Hall|Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center]].}}]] The iron artifact burial facility was arranged in two rows: east and west. The eastern row measured 5.2 meters in length and 70–80 cm in width, containing over 232 iron burial goods, including [[tachi]] swords, {{ill|tsurugi|ja|剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsurugi|1=Double-edged sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396047}} swords, short swords, spears, and halberds. The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.
=== Haniwa and Dating === A row of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} was unearthed, with 16 pieces discovered on the north side and 31 on the south. Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.
=== Function of the Kofun === The absence of a burial chamber suggests that Nishihakayama Kofun may have served as a ceremonial satellite mound (fukusōyō baizuka) rather than a primary burial site. Similar satellite mounds are known in the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}}, such as {{ill|Katomboyama Kofun|ja|カトンボ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katomboyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsumakairi Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|1=Tsukamawari Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumakairi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakamiyama Kofun|ja|七観山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, as well as in the Furuichi Kofun Group, such as {{ill|Ariyama Kofun|ja|アリ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ariyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nochū Kofun|ja|野中古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nochū Kofun|qq=}}. Although Nishihakayama Kofun no longer exists, a section of the iron artifact burial facility was preserved and is now exhibited at the {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|1=AICEL Shura Hall|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aisel Shura Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}} (officially, Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center).
== References == * Nishihakayama Kofun: Furuichi Kofun Group Research Report III, Fujidera City Board of Education, 1997.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/9,0,98,146.html About Nishihakayama Kofun] (Fujidera City)

The mound is presumed to have been square-shaped and is one of the kofun that typeed part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}}, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Historic Site site|1=World Heritage Site}}.

The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.

Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Nishihakayama Kofun |image= |location={{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|1=Fujiidera}} |位置= |type=Square-shaped kofun |area=Approximately20m per side |built=5th century |Burial Facility=Iron artifact burial facility |Interred Persons= |burial goods=Numerous iron burial goods |Historic Site Designation= |Special Notes=Buried kofun |architectural_styles={{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353|1=Square Kofun}} |part_of={{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}} }} '''Nishihakayama Kofun''' (西墓山古墳) was a [[kofun]] (ancient Kofun) located in Aoyama, [[Fujidera City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The mound is presumed to have been square-shaped and is one of the kofun that typeed part of the {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Group|1=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}}, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Historic Site site|1=World Heritage Site}}.
The existence of the kofun was confirmed during an excavation conducted due to redevelopment for residential housing. A row of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} and an iron artifact burial structure were discovered, revealing a wealth of iron burial goods.
== Overview == The kofun was located west of the {{ill|Makuyama Kofun|ja|墓山古墳|1=Hakayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makuyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakayama Kofun|qq=}}'s front section and just south of its associated {{ill|Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja|浄元寺山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgenjiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a satellite mound. In 1988, plans for replacing a wooden apartment complex with a reinforced concrete building prompted an excavation. Although no remains of the mound or related structures had been identified in this area, its proximity to Jōgenjiyama Kofun suggested the possibility of associated features such as moats or embankments, warranting a preliminary investigation. Shortly after the excavation began, a row of haniwa and an iron artifact burial facility were discovered.
=== Internal burial === [[File:Nishihakayama Kofun Shutaibu.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main burial area}}{{small|Exhibited at the [[AICEL Shura Hall|Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center]].}}]] The iron artifact burial facility was arranged in two rows: east and west. The eastern row measured 5.2 meters in length and 70–80 cm in width, containing over 232 iron burial goods, including [[tachi]] swords, {{ill|tsurugi|ja|剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsurugi|1=Double-edged sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396047}} swords, short swords, spears, and halberds. The western row measured 6.2 meters in length and 60–80 cm in width, containing over 1,168 iron tools and weapons such as short swords, stabbing implements, plow tips, axes, knives, chisels, awls, saws, and more. Additionally, over 10 stone burial goods in the type of axes and at least one sickle-shaped stone artifact were found.
Analysis of the iron tools revealed that they were primarily ceremonial items. Plow tips, for example, often lacked functional edges, and knives were found to be imitations rather than practical tools. These findings suggest that the burial goods were created specifically for burial rituals and were not intended for practical use.
=== Haniwa and Dating === A row of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} was unearthed, with 16 pieces discovered on the north side and 31 on the south. Numerous fragments of cylindrical, morning-glory-shaped, and figurative haniwa were also excavated. The cylindrical haniwa were encircled by triple bands, with a diameter of approximately 30 cm. Only one piece was complete, standing at 53.4 cm in height. The kofun is dated to the Kawanishi III sub-phase (early to mid-5th century). Figurative haniwa fragments included lid-shaped and house-shaped varieties.
The mound itself had been mostly leveled due to residential development. Aerial photographs from 1941 show the area already being used for farming, indicating that the mound was destroyed before that time.
=== Function of the Kofun === The absence of a burial chamber suggests that Nishihakayama Kofun may have served as a ceremonial satellite mound (fukusōyō baizuka) rather than a primary burial site. Similar satellite mounds are known in the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs|1=Mozu Tombs}}, such as {{ill|Katomboyama Kofun|ja|カトンボ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katomboyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsumakairi Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|1=Tsukamawari Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumakairi Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakunoyama Kofun|lt=Nanakamiyama Kofun|ja|七観山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, as well as in the Furuichi Kofun Group, such as {{ill|Ariyama Kofun|ja|アリ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ariyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nonaka Kofun|lt=Nochū Kofun|ja|野中古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nochū Kofun|qq=}}. Although Nishihakayama Kofun no longer exists, a section of the iron artifact burial facility was preserved and is now exhibited at the {{ill|AICEL Shura Hall|ja|アイセルシュラホール|1=AICEL Shura Hall|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aisel Shura Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/AICEL Shura Hall|qq=}} (officially, Fujidera City Lifelong Learning Center).
== References == * Nishihakayama Kofun: Furuichi Kofun Group Research Report III, Fujidera City Board of Education, 1997.
